seo_description: Bizango are the fierce Petwo spirits and secret societies of Haitian Vodou, feared for their power to enforce justice through supernatural means including zombification.
source: Haitian Vodou oral tradition, Haiti folklore

name: Bouki (Haitian Trickster Hyena)
description: Bouki is the slow-witted, greedy hyena trickster of Haitian folklore -- the eternal foil to the clever Rabbit (or Malis/Connen, the rabbit trickster). In classic Haitian folktales, Bouki is always scheming for an easy meal or some treasure, and he is always outsmarted by the clever rabbit, suffering humiliation or injury. Bouki represents the dangers of greed, gluttony, and stupidity. Unlike the clever Anansi or the sneaky Malis rabbit, Bouki thinks he is cunning but is fundamentally foolish. His name derives from the Wolof word mbouki (hyena) from Senegal, proving his West African origins. Bouki tales were brought to Haiti by enslaved Senegambians and remain a staple of Haitian oral tradition -- they are told in Haitian Creole (Kreyol) and used to teach children moral lessons about generosity, honesty, and the consequences of greed. The name Bouki is a common Creole insult in Haiti, meaning someone foolish or easily tricked.
appearance: A hyena -- depicted as shabby, hungry, perpetually scheming, and somewhat pathetic. In folklore illustrations: long snout, patchy fur, hunched back, greedy eyes. Often shown wearing rags or carrying a sack for his imagined treasures.
behavior: Greedy, gluttonous, lazy, and foolish. Constantly schemes for easy food and wealth. Believes he is clever but is inevitably outwitted by the rabbit. Falls for obvious traps. Never learns from his mistakes.
habitat: Haitian countryside -- rural villages, farms, forests, market roads
cultural_significance: Bouki and Rabbit (Malis/Connen) tales form the most important cycle of Haitian folklore. They are West African in origin (Wolof mbouki = hyena) adapted to the Haitian context. These stories served enslaved Africans as a way to secretly critique the plantation system -- Bouki represents the foolish, greedy master, while the clever Rabbit represents the enslaved person who wins through wit. Bouki name has entered the Haitian Creole vocabulary as an insult meaning fool or blockhead.
summary: Bouki is the greedy, foolhardy hyena trickster of Haitian folklore who is always outsmarted by the clever Rabbit
fun_fact: Bouki and Rabbit (Malis/Connen) tales form the most important cycle of Haitian folklore. They are West African in origin (Wolof mbouki = hyena) adapted to the Haitian context. These stories served enslaved Africans as a way to secretly critique the plantation system -- Bouki represents the foolish, greedy master, while the clever Rabbit represents the enslaved person who wins through wit. Bouki name has entered the Haitian Creole vocabulary as an insult meaning fool or blockhead.
seo_description: Bouki is the greedy, foolhardy hyena trickster of Haitian folklore who is always outsmarted by the clever Rabbit.
source: Haitian oral tradition, Haiti folklore

name: Buraq (Celestial Steed)
description: Buraq is the fabulous celestial steed that carried the Prophet Muhammad from Mecca to Jerusalem and through the seven heavens during the Night Journey (Isra and Mi'raj) in 621 CE. Described as a wingèd creature, white and luminous like lightning, larger than a donkey but smaller than a mule. Its step stretches as far as the eye can see, covering impossible distances in moments. In Near Eastern and Persian art, Buraq is depicted with the head of a beautiful woman, the body of a horse, and the tail of a peacock. The name Buraq derives from the Arabic root for 'lightning', reflecting its phenomenal speed. The creature is also associated with other prophets before Muhammad, including Ibrahim (Abraham).
appearance: A shining white creature with the beautiful face of a woman, body of a horse, two large wings on its thighs, a peacock's tail, and a brilliant luminous quality like lightning.
behavior: Obedient, swift beyond comprehension, and celestial. Carries prophets across impossible distances. Soars through the heavens with grace and power. Responds only to divine command.
habitat: The heavens, the celestial realm between earth and paradise
cultural_significance: Buraq is one of the most revered creatures in Islamic tradition, central to the Isra and Mi'raj (the Night Journey and Ascension). The journey is commemorated annually as Laylat al-Miraj. Buraq symbolizes the link between the earthly and the divine, and remains a powerful symbol of Islamic spirituality and mystical ascent.
summary: The legendary winged steed that carried the Prophet Muhammad through the seven heavens during the Night Journey.
fun_fact: The legendary winged steed that carried the Prophet Muhammad through the seven heavens during the Night Journey.
seo_description: The legendary winged steed that carried the Prophet Muhammad through the seven heavens during the Night Journey.
source: Quran, Hadith, Islamic tradition

name: Bahamut (Cosmic Fish)
description: Bahamut is a colossal cosmic fish in Arabic mythology that supports the entire structure of the world. According to medieval Islamic cosmology, Bahamut is so vast that all the oceans of the world placed in one of its nostrils would be like a mustard seed in the desert. Upon Bahamut's head stands a giant bull named Kuyutha (or Kiyuban) with 40,000 horns and legs, upon whose back rests a mountain of ruby, atop which stands an angel who supports the seven heavens and the earth. Below Bahamut lies a fathomless sea, beneath that a realm of air, then a realm of fire, and finally the great serpent Falak. The fish causes earthquakes when it stirs. Bahamut appears in the 496th night of the Thousand and One Nights, witnessed by Prophet Isa (Jesus).
appearance: An unimaginably vast cosmic fish, often described with the head of a hippopotamus or elephant, covered in scales, with horns like a bull. So enormous that human senses cannot comprehend its size.
behavior: Eternal, passive, foundational. Supports the world on its back. Causes earthquakes when it moves or becomes restless. Mostly dormant in the cosmic ocean.
habitat: The cosmic ocean beneath the world, the lowest depths of creation
cultural_significance: Bahamut is a central figure in classical Arabic cosmology, representing the foundation of the physical universe. The story appears in al-Qazwini's Wonders of Creation and the Thousand and One Nights. The name Bahamut is derived from Behemoth in Biblical tradition, transformed into a cosmic fish in Arabic lore.
summary: A colossal cosmic fish in Arabic mythology that supports the world on its back, so vast that all oceans are like a mustard seed in its nostril.
fun_fact: Bahamut is a central figure in classical Arabic cosmology, representing the foundation of the physical universe. The story appears in al-Qazwini's Wonders of Creation and the Thousand and One Nights. The name Bahamut is derived from Behemoth in Biblical tradition, transformed into a cosmic fish in Arabic lore.
seo_description: A colossal cosmic fish in Arabic mythology that supports the world on its back, so vast that all oceans are like a mustard seed in its nostril.
source: al-Qazwini - Wonders of Creation; Thousand and One Nights

name: Bennu (Egyptian Phoenix)
description: The Bennu is the sacred heron-like bird of Egyptian mythology, the original Egyptian phoenix. It was the ba (soul) of Ra and the soul of Osiris. The Bennu is associated with creation; snip it alighted on the primordial mound (Benben) at the first sunrise, breaking the silence of the primeval waters with its cry. The Benben stone, the pyramidal capstone of obelisks, represents the mound upon which the Bennu perched. It also represents the annual flooding of the Nile and the cycle of death and rebirth. The Greeks later identified the Bennu with their own phoenix, but the Egyptian version predates the Greek phoenix by centuries. The Bennu is closely associated with the city of Heliopolis.
appearance: A large grey heron with a blue-grey plumage, two long trailing feathers on its head, and sometimes a golden or red crest. Occasionally depicted with a human head or crowned with the Atef crown of Osiris.
behavior: Solitary, sacred, and deeply symbolic. Its cry began creation. Cycles of death and rebirth. Associated with the sunrise and the inundation. Wise and ancient.
habitat: Heliopolis, the Benben mound, the willow tree at the edge of the underworld
cultural_significance: The Bennu is the direct predecessor of the Greek phoenix myth. It is the symbol of resurrection in Egyptian culture, representing the annual flooding of the Nile and Osiris's resurrection. The Bennu hieroglyph appears in the word for 'to rise in brilliance'. It was worshipped at Heliopolis alongside Ra.
summary: The sacred heron of Egyptian mythology that alighted on the primordial mound at creation, the original phoenix of world mythology.
fun_fact: The Bennu is the direct predecessor of the Greek phoenix myth. It is the symbol of resurrection in Egyptian culture, representing the annual flooding of the Nile and Osiris's resurrection. The Bennu hieroglyph appears in the word for 'to rise in brilliance'. It was worshipped at Heliopolis alongside Ra.
seo_description: The sacred heron of Egyptian mythology that alighted on the primordial mound at creation, the original phoenix of world mythology.
source: Egyptian mythology, Pyramid Texts, Heliopolitan tradition, Herodotus

name: Bahri Spirits (Moroccan Sea Jinn)
description: Bahri spirits (from Arabic 'bahr' meaning sea) are a class of water-dwelling jinn in Moroccan folklore that inhabit the Atlantic Ocean and the Mediterranean Sea along the Moroccan coast. They are considered the most dangerous of all jinn in Moroccan belief, as they are completely alien to human nature and do not share the terrestrial realm with humans. They appear as half-fish, half-human creatures with green eyes, scaled skin, and webbed hands and feet. Bahri spirits are blamed for shipwrecks, drownings, and disappearing fishermen. They enchant sailors with beautiful songs (like the Greek Sirens) or drag swimmers underwater. They are most active during storms and at sunset. Some traditions say they are led by a king named Sakhr or reside under the sea in crystal palaces. They are unique among jinn for being unable to cross fresh water.
appearance: Half-human, half-fish creatures with greenish-blue scaled skin, large round yellow eyes, webbed fingers and toes, gills on the neck, and long green hair like seaweed. Can appear as beautiful humans.
behavior: Hostile and predatory towards humans. Drown sailors and swimmers. Lure with songs. Curious about human artifacts. Active during storms. Cannot survive in fresh water.
habitat: The Atlantic Ocean, the Mediterranean Sea, coastal caves, underwater ruins
weaknesses: vulnerable to salt from the desert (baraka)
cultural_significance: Bahri spirits reflect the deep fear and respect coastal Moroccans have for the sea. Unlike desert jinn, these beings are seen as irredeemably hostile. Fishing communities have elaborate rituals to avoid attracting Bahri spirits, including prohibitions on whistling at sea and specific prayers before launching boats.
summary: Dangerous sea-dwelling jinn from Moroccan folklore that inhabit the Atlantic and Mediterranean coasts, drowning sailors and luring swimmers to their deaths.
fun_fact: Bahri spirits reflect the deep fear and respect coastal Moroccans have for the sea. Unlike desert jinn, these beings are seen as irredeemably hostile. Fishing communities have elaborate rituals to avoid attracting Bahri spirits, including prohibitions on whistling at sea and specific prayers before launching boats.
seo_title: Bahri Spirits (Moroccan Sea Jinn) – Moroccan Folklore
seo_description: Dangerous sea-dwelling jinn from Moroccan folklore that inhabit the Atlantic and Mediterranean coasts, drowning sailors and luring swimmers to their deaths.
source: Moroccan oral tradition, Maghrebi Islamic folklore

name: Basilisk
description: Basilisk is a dragon from European/Arabic mythology. Described by Pliny the Elder and later Christian bestiaries, the Basilisk is a small but deadly serpent crown-crested creature whose mere gaze causes death. It is said to be hatched from a cock's egg incubated by a serpent or toad. In Christian symbology adopted by the Vatican, the Basilisk represents the power of sin and heresy that can be overcome by faith. Medieval bestiaries often used the Basilisk as a symbol of the Antichrist, and its deadly gaze represented the corrupting power of evil.
appearance: A serpentine or reptilian creature with a crown-like crest on its head, scales that shimmer with an iridescent sheen, and eyes that glow with a pale, deadly light. It is smaller than a dragon but carries an aura of lethal danger.
behavior: Possesses the power to kill with a single glance or its breath. Its presence causes vegetation to wither and stones to crack. It is a lethal predator that avoids confrontation with its reflection, which is its only weakness, as seeing itself mirrors its deadly gaze back upon it.
cultural_significance: Represents the hidden and sudden nature of death, and the idea that small things can possess overwhelming power.
summary: Legendary serpent king known in Vatican tradition for its lethal gaze, symbolizing the power of sin overcome by Christian faith
fun_fact: The Basilisk is a legendary serpent king in Vatican and Christian bestiary tradition, whose lethal gaze symbolizes the power of sin overcome by faith
seo_description: The Basilisk is a legendary serpent king in Vatican and Christian bestiary tradition, whose lethal gaze symbolizes the power of sin overcome by faith
source: European and Arabic folklore, Pliny the Elder

name: Boto
description: Boto is a shapeshifter from Brazil folklore. Also known as encantado. It is said to dwell in Amazon River. In folklore, it is described as he attends parties and festivals, seduces women, and then returns to the river at dawn, leaving the woman pregnant. A myth often used to explain unplanned pregnancies in river communities. The shapeshifting Amazon river dolphin. Discover the legend of the Boto and his white hat.
appearance: Usually appears as a charming man dressed in white, always wearing a hat to hide the blowhole on top of his head.
behavior: He attends parties and festivals, seduces women, and then returns to the river at dawn, leaving the woman pregnant.
cultural_significance: A myth often used to explain unplanned pregnancies in river communities.
summary: Boto is a shapeshifter from the folklore and mythology of Brazil.
fun_fact: The shapeshifting Amazon river dolphin. Discover the legend of the Boto and his white hat.
seo_description: The shapeshifting Amazon river dolphin. Discover the legend of the Boto and his white hat.
source: Brazilian folklore

name: Barong
description: A lion-like creature and the king of spirits, representing the force of good and balance in the universe.
appearance: A large, shaggy creature resembling a cross between a lion and a dog, often worn by two dancers.
behavior: He protects humans from the evil influence of Rangda and maintains cosmic balance through dance and ritual.
cultural_significance: Central to Balinese mythology and the 'Barong Dance' ritual performance.
summary: Barong is a spirit from the folklore and mythology of Indonesia.
fun_fact: The Barong: the lion-like guardian of Bali. Learn about the protector of the spiritual balance.
seo_description: The Barong: the lion-like guardian of Bali. Learn about the protector of the spiritual balance.
source: Indonesian folklore

name: Bajlozi
description: Bajlozi is a giant from Albania folklore. Also known as shadow giant. It is said to dwell in High mountain gates, dark valleys. In folklore, it is described as blocks mountain passes and demands riddles or tributes from travelers. Represents the 'big' obstacles of life that require wit rather than brute force.
appearance: A massive, imposing figure made of living shadow, with glowing eyes.
behavior: Blocks mountain passes and demands riddles or tributes from travelers.
cultural_significance: Represents the 'big' obstacles of life that require wit rather than brute force.
summary: Bajlozi is a giant from Albanian folklore in Albania, believed to embody the spiritual traditions of the region.
fun_fact: Represents the 'big' obstacles of life that require wit rather than brute force.
seo_description: The Bajlozi: the towering shadow giant of Albanian folklore.
source: Albanian folklore

name: Belu
description: Belu is a ogre from Myanmar folklore. It is said to dwell in Deep jungles, caves. In folklore, it is described as territorial and aggressive; some can shapeshift into humans to deceive their prey. Represents the untamed nature of the wild and the possibility of spiritual redemption.
